title: How herbal infusions work
subtitle: The extraction science behind the covered cup
excerpt: What actually happens when you steep herbs in hot water, why the lid matters, what the steep time does, why some plants need cold water instead of hot, and how to read a recipe that says 'infusion' versus 'decoction'. The practical foundations for every hot herb preparation.
sourceNotes: Maud Grieve, A Modern Herbal (1931), general introduction, infusion and decoction methods described. Traditional apothecary practice as documented across European pharmacopoeia texts. EMA HMPC guidance on herbal preparations, definitions of infusion, decoction, cold maceration.

content.text: An
infusion

is what most people call a herb tea. Hot water poured over plant material, covered, and left to steep. Simple, but the details matter more than most people realise. This article explains the mechanics behind the covered cup, the steep time, and the choice between hot and cold water, so that every infusion recipe in this section makes sense rather than just being a set of steps to follow.
Why the lid is not optional
Every infusion recipe in this section says to put the lid on immediately. This is the single most important instruction in herbal infusion preparation and the most commonly ignored. The reason is
volatile oils

, the scented part of the herb that carries much of its working action. Volatile oils are volatile by name: they boil off easily and rise out with the steam. An uncovered cup of chamomile tea loses its calming bisabolol into the kitchen air within the first minute of steeping. The cup that arrives at the table smells of chamomile but has lost most of its working value. The lid keeps the volatile oils in. A saucer over the cup does the same job if you do not have a lid.
Why steep time matters

The standard steep time for most herbal infusions is 10 minutes. That is long enough to draw out the main active parts of flowers and leaves. It is short enough to keep most of the bitter tannins behind. Steep for only two or three minutes and you get a nice-tasting but weak cup. The volatile oils are out, but the slower-releasing parts are still in the plant. Steep for more than 15 minutes and the cup turns bitter as more tannins come through, without adding much active strength.
Hot infusion versus simmered roots

Flowers and leaves, chamomile, lavender, lemon balm, elderflower, nettle, peppermint, are delicate enough to release their constituents into hot water without requiring sustained heat. A 10-minute infusion works. Roots, bark, seeds, and berries, valerian root, elderberries, fennel seeds, are denser and tougher. Their cell walls do not break open at steeping temperature. A
decoction

, a steady simmer in a covered pan for 20 to 40 minutes, is needed. Fennel seeds are a middle case. Lightly crush them first to break the seed coat, and hot-water infusion works without full simmering. That is why the fennel tea recipe calls for crushing the seeds first.
When cold water works better than hot

Marshmallow root is the exception that proves the rule. Its primary therapeutic compound is mucilage, a polysaccharide gel that swells in water to coat and soothe mucous membranes. Heat destroys the mucilage. A hot infusion of marshmallow root produces a thinner, more bitter liquid than a
cold infusion

, the cold water slowly draws out the mucilage over eight to twelve hours without destroying it. If a recipe in this section specifies cold water and overnight steeping, this is the reason: the active constituent requires cold extraction.
Water temperature

The standard instruction is 'just off the boil'. That means water that has been boiled and then left to stand for 30 to 60 seconds, so it comes down to around 90 to 95°C. Hot enough to draw out the herb. Not so hot that it flashes the volatile oils off on contact. Water poured straight from a rolling boil is a touch hotter than ideal for delicate flowers. The difference is small. The 30-second wait is not a ritual; it has a small real effect.
What 'infusion' means on a label

The term 'infusion' in a recipe heading means: pour hot water over, cover, steep 10 minutes, strain. It does not mean simmer, boil, or cook. A simmered preparation is the next method along: simmer covered for 20 to 40 minutes. 'Cold infusion' or 'cold steeping' means cold water, no heat, steep overnight. The three methods are not interchangeable. Use the wrong one and you get a cup that is either weaker or more bitter than intended. With marshmallow root, the wrong method drops the main working compound entirely.
